Pose.safety: Do not force your knees toward the floor—let gravity do the work over time. If you have groin, hip, or knee injuries, place blocks or folded blankets under your thighs for support. Avoid this pose if you have a recent or severe groin injury. Pregnant women in later stages should practice with caution and avoid deep forward folds.
Pose.Name: Titli Asana (Butterfly Pose / Baddha Konasana)
Pose.Equipment: Yoga Mat, Optional: Blocks or Folded Blankets for Support

Focused_Muscles.Primary: Hip Adductors (Inner Thighs / Groin), Hip Flexors
Focused_Muscles.Secondary: Lower Back (Erector Spinae), Pelvic Floor Muscles, Quadriceps

Pose.Preferred_Time: Morning (to wake up the hips), Evening (to release hip tension), or after a workout (cool-down stretch)

Diseases_Cured.Targeted: Tight Hip Adductors (Groin), Lower Back Tension, Poor Hip Mobility, Sciatica (mild)
Diseases_Cured.Preventive: Improves hip flexibility, relieves menstrual discomfort, aids digestion (massages abdominal organs), prepares hips for childbirth

steps.step: Sit on your mat with your legs extended straight in front of you. If your lower back rounds, sit on a folded blanket or block to tilt your pelvis forward. Keep your spine long and chest lifted. Engage your core slightly.

Bend your knees and bring the soles of your feet together, allowing your knees to drop out to the sides. Draw your heels as close to your pelvis as comfortable—do not force. Your feet should be held gently in your hands.
Gently hold your feet or ankles with your hands. Keep your shoulders relaxed and away from your ears. Engage your core slightly to maintain a long spine. Press your sit bones firmly into the mat.

For the dynamic "butterfly" movement: Gently press your knees up and down using your inner thigh muscles (not your hands). Move slowly and rhythmically like a butterfly flapping its wings. Perform 15-20 gentle flaps while breathing naturally.

step.focus: Static Hold (Baddha Konasana) - Optional Forward Fold

steps.step: To hold the pose statically: Keep your knees dropped toward the floor. On an inhale, lengthen your spine. On an exhale, gently hinge forward from your hips, keeping your back straight. Only go as far as you feel a stretch—no pain. Hold for 5-10 deep breaths. To release, inhale and slowly lift your torso back up.

Imagine your knees are heavy and melting toward the floor. Do not push them down with your hands—let gravity and your breath do the work. For a deeper stretch, place a block under each knee for support. If you have knee issues, place rolled blankets behind your knees.

common_mistakes.mistake: Forcing the knees down with your hands or elbows (this can strain the groin).
Rounding the lower back instead of hinging from the hips when folding forward.
Lifting the shoulders toward the ears (keep them relaxed and down).
Bouncing the knees aggressively during the dynamic version (move gently and slowly).
Holding the breath—breathe deeply and steadily throughout.

Pose.safety: Move slowly with your breath. Do not force the movement—let your spine move naturally. If you have neck injuries, keep your head in a neutral position rather than looking up or down. Avoid this pose if you have severe carpal tunnel syndrome or recent abdominal surgery. If you have wrist pain, make fists instead of keeping palms flat.
Pose.Name: Cat-Cow Pose (Marjaryasana-Bitilasana / Chakravakasana)

Focused_Muscles.Secondary: Glutes, Hamstrings, Shoulders, Chest, Pelvic Floor

Pose.Preferred_Time: Morning (to wake up the spine), Before Bed (to release tension), or as a Warm-Up for any workout or yoga session

Diseases_Cured.Targeted: Lower Back Stiffness, Neck Tension, Poor Spinal Mobility, Posture Issues, Stress and Anxiety
Diseases_Cured.Preventive: Improves spinal flexibility and circulation, relieves tension headaches, promotes healthy digestion (massages abdominal organs), warms up the back for exercise, reduces stress through breath coordination

steps.step: Begin on your hands and knees in a tabletop position. Place your hands directly under your shoulders and your knees directly under your hips. Keep your back flat and neutral. Spread your fingers wide for stability. Align your head and neck with your spine—gaze at the floor.

Inhale deeply as you slowly drop your belly toward the floor. Lift your sit bones and chest toward the ceiling. Gently lift your gaze upward (without straining your neck—keep it as a natural extension of the spine). Draw your shoulder blades together and down your back. Feel a gentle stretch in your chest and belly.

Exhale fully as you round your spine toward the ceiling like a stretching cat. Tuck your tailbone under. Draw your belly button toward your spine. Gently drop your head and gaze toward your navel. Feel a stretch through your upper, middle, and lower back. Keep your shoulders relaxed.

Continue flowing between Cow Pose on each inhale and Cat Pose on each exhale. Move slowly and deliberately, coordinating each movement with your breath. Perform 8-12 complete rounds (one round = one inhale Cow + one exhale Cat). Let the movement become meditative.
After completing your rounds, return to a neutral tabletop position with a flat back. Take a few natural breaths. Notice how your spine feels—more mobile, relaxed, and awake.

Let your breath guide the movement—don't rush. The inhale naturally opens the chest (Cow), and the exhale naturally rounds the spine (Cat). Imagine each vertebra moving one by one as you flow. If you have wrist discomfort, make fists instead of keeping palms flat, or use a folded mat under your wrists.

common_mistakes.mistake: Moving too quickly without coordinating with breath.
Forcing the neck to look too far up or down (keep it as a natural extension of the spine).
Locking the elbows (keep a micro-bend to protect the joints).
Holding tension in the shoulders instead of letting them relax and move with the spine.
Holding the breath—breathe deeply and steadily throughout the flow.

Pose.safety: This is an intense hip opener. Do not force your knees or ankles. If you have knee or ankle injuries, avoid this pose or practice with significant support (blankets under the knees). Listen to your body—discomfort is okay, but sharp pain means stop. Practice on a soft surface.
Pose.Name: Double Pigeon Pose (Agnistambhasana / Fire Log Pose)
Pose.Equipment: Yoga Mat, Optional: Blocks or Folded Blankets

Focused_Muscles.Primary: External Hip Rotators (Piriformis, Gluteal Muscles), Hip Adductors

Pose.Preferred_Time: Evening (to release deep hip tension), or after a workout (cool-down stretch)

Diseases_Cured.Targeted: Tight Hips, Piriformis Syndrome, Sciatica (mild to moderate), Lower Back Tension
Diseases_Cured.Preventive: Improves hip mobility for sitting and walking, relieves tension from prolonged sitting, prepares hips for meditation postures, reduces risk of hip injuries

steps.step: Sit on your mat with your legs extended straight. If your lower back rounds, sit on a folded blanket or block to tilt your pelvis forward. Stack your spine tall, shoulders relaxed, chest open.

Bend your right knee and place your right shin on the mat parallel to the front edge of your mat. Your right ankle should be in front of your left hip, and your right knee in front of your right hip. The outer edge of your right shin and foot rests on the mat.

Bend your left knee and place your left shin on top of your right shin. Stack your left ankle directly above your right knee, and your left knee directly above your right ankle. Your shins should be stacked like logs on a fire.

Your knees and ankles should be stacked vertically. If there is a large gap or significant discomfort, place a folded blanket or block between your shins for support. Flex both feet to protect your ankles.

On an inhale, lengthen your spine. On an exhale, gently hinge forward from your hips, keeping your back straight. Walk your hands forward on the mat. Only go as far as you feel a deep stretch in your outer hips—no sharp pain. Hold for 5-10 breaths. To release, inhale and slowly lift your torso. Unstack your legs one at a time. Repeat on the opposite side (left shin on bottom, right shin on top).

Double Pigeon is an intense stretch. Start with just 1-2 minutes on each side. Use blankets under your sit bones and between your shins for support. If you cannot stack your shins, practice with only the first leg (Single Pigeon) or place a block between your knees. Flex your feet to protect your ankles.

common_mistakes.mistake: Forcing the stacked shin position when hips are not ready (use support instead).
Rounding the lower back when folding forward (hinge from the hips).
Letting the top foot point (keep the foot flexed to protect the ankle).
Holding the breath—breathe deeply into the sensation.
Staying in the pose too long if there is sharp pain (discomfort is okay, pain is not).

Pose.safety: Practice in a quiet, comfortable space where you won't be disturbed. If you feel lightheaded or dizzy, return to normal breathing. Do not force your breath—let it be natural and easy. Avoid this practice if you have severe respiratory conditions without consulting a doctor.
Pose.Name: Mindful Breathing Meditation (Pranayama / Dirga Swasam)
Pose.Equipment: Yoga Mat, Meditation Cushion or Folded Blanket (Optional), Quiet Space

Focused_Muscles.Secondary: Abdominal Muscles (Minor), Pelvic Floor, Respiratory Muscles
Focused_Muscles.Stabilizers: No major stabilizers—this is a seated, restful practice

Pose.Preferred_Time: Morning (to start the day with calm), Evening (to unwind before sleep), or Anytime you feel stressed or overwhelmed

Diseases_Cured.Targeted: Stress, Anxiety, Insomnia, High Blood Pressure (mild to moderate), Racing Thoughts
Diseases_Cured.Preventive: Improves lung capacity, reduces cortisol (stress hormone), lowers heart rate and blood pressure, enhances focus and concentration, promotes emotional regulation, strengthens the mind-body connection

steps.step: Sit in a comfortable cross-legged position (Sukhasana) on a cushion or folded blanket to elevate your hips slightly above your knees. Alternatively, sit on a chair with your feet flat on the floor and hands resting on your thighs. Keep your spine tall but not rigid—natural curves preserved.

Gently close your eyes or soften your gaze toward the floor. Relax your shoulders away from your ears. Rest your hands on your knees or thighs, palms up (receptive) or palms down (grounding). Unclench your jaw and relax your tongue.

Without changing your breath, simply notice it. Feel the sensation of the breath entering through your nostrils, filling your lungs, and leaving your body. Notice the coolness of the inhale and the warmth of the exhale. Do this for 1-2 minutes.

To anchor your attention, count each exhale: Inhale naturally, then as you exhale, silently count "one." Next exhale, count "two." Continue up to "five," then start back at "one." If you lose count, simply begin again at "one" without judgment.

When your mind wanders (and it will—this is normal), gently notice where it went without criticism. Softly return your attention to the sensation of your breath. Each time you return, you are strengthening your mindfulness muscle. Practice for 5-10 minutes to start.

Consistency is more important than duration. Even 2-3 minutes of mindful breathing daily is beneficial. Use a timer so you don't watch the clock. If you feel anxious focusing on your breath, try focusing on the feeling of your hands resting on your thighs instead. There is no "perfect" meditation—just showing up is enough.

common_mistakes.mistake: Expecting the mind to be completely still (this is unrealistic—thoughts are normal).
Judging yourself for having wandering thoughts (gently return without criticism).
Forcing or controlling the breath (let it be natural and easy).
Slouching or collapsing the chest (keep a tall, open posture for better breathing).
Giving up when it feels "boring" or "hard" (these feelings are part of the practice).

Pose.safety: This is an inversion. Do not practice if you have high blood pressure (uncontrolled), glaucoma, retinal detachment, neck or spine injuries, herniated discs, are menstruating, or in late pregnancy (2nd/3rd trimester). If you have thyroid issues, practice with caution. Always come out of the pose if you feel any neck discomfort. Use folded blankets under your shoulders to protect your neck.
Pose.Name: Shoulder Stand Pose (Sarvangasana - "All Limbs Pose")
Pose.Equipment: Yoga Mat, 2-3 Folded Blankets (to protect the neck), Wall (Optional for support)

Focused_Muscles.Secondary: Thyroid Gland (Stimulation), Parathyroid Glands, Abdominal Organs

Pose.Preferred_Time: Morning or Early Evening, on an Empty Stomach (wait 2-3 hours after a meal)

Diseases_Cured.Targeted: Poor Circulation, Fatigue, Insomnia, Thyroid Imbalance (mild), Sinus Congestion (drainage)
Diseases_Cured.Preventive: Improves blood flow to the brain and upper body, stimulates the thyroid and parathyroid glands, strengthens shoulders and core, calms the nervous system, relieves mild depression and anxiety, improves digestion (through gentle pressure)

steps.step: Fold 2-3 blankets into a firm stack (about 1-2 inches thick). Place the blankets on your mat near the center. Lie on your back with your shoulders on the blankets and your head resting on the mat. Your head should be lower than your shoulders to create a slight chin-to-chest position.
Lie on your back with your arms alongside your body, palms down. Press your palms into the mat. Inhale as you lift your legs straight up to 90 degrees (like a reverse leg lift). Engage your core.

Exhale as you press your palms down and lift your hips off the floor, rolling your spine up. Support your lower back with your hands (palms on your back, fingers pointing upward). Walk your hands toward your shoulder blades.

Inhale as you slowly straighten your legs and torso upward. Your weight should rest on your shoulders and upper arms—NOT on your neck. Your hips should be aligned over your shoulders, and your toes pointing toward the ceiling. Keep your elbows shoulder-width apart.

Breathe slowly and steadily through your nose. Hold the pose for 30 seconds to 3 minutes depending on your experience. Keep your gaze toward your chest (not turning your head). Engage your core to keep the body stable.

To exit, exhale as you slowly roll your spine back down to the floor, vertebra by vertebra. Lower your legs to the floor when your hips touch the mat. Rest in Corpse Pose (Savasana) for at least 30 seconds to neutralize your spine.

Never turn your head while in Shoulder Stand—this can injure your neck. Keep your weight on your shoulders, not your neck. If you feel any neck pressure, come out immediately. Beginners should practice with a wall for support or learn from an experienced teacher. After Shoulder Stand, practice Fish Pose (Matsyasana) as a counterpose to release the neck.

common_mistakes.mistake: Putting weight on the neck instead of the shoulders (use blankets and keep weight on shoulders).
Turning the head while in the pose (this can cause serious neck injury).
Elbows slipping too wide apart (keep elbows shoulder-width and press down).
Rushing the exit—roll down slowly and with control.
Holding the breath (breathe deeply and steadily).

Pose.safety: Practice near a wall for balance support. Do not place your foot directly on your knee joint (always place it above or below the knee). If you have high or low blood pressure, raise your arms only when stable. Avoid this pose if you have recent hip, knee, or ankle injuries.
Pose.Name: Tree Pose (Vrksasana)
Pose.Equipment: Yoga Mat, Wall (Optional for balance support)

Focused_Muscles.Primary: Standing Leg: Quadriceps, Glutes, Calves, Foot Intrinsics (Balance)
Focused_Muscles.Secondary: Lifted Leg: Hip Abductors (Tensor Fasciae Latae, Gluteus Medius), Hip Flexors
Focused_Muscles.Stabilizers: Core (Transverse Abdominis, Obliques), Spine, Shoulders (if arms are raised)

Pose.Preferred_Time: Morning (to build focus and balance for the day), or as a standing pose in any yoga sequence

Diseases_Cured.Targeted: Poor Balance, Weak Ankles, Lack of Focus, Sciatica (mild)
Diseases_Cured.Preventive: Improves proprioception (body awareness), strengthens ankles and knees, builds mental focus and concentration, opens the hips, improves posture, reduces flat feet over time

steps.step: Begin standing in Mountain Pose (Tadasana) with your feet together or hip-width apart. Distribute your weight evenly across both feet. Engage your thighs, lift your kneecaps, and tuck your tailbone slightly. Lengthen your spine and relax your shoulders.
Shift your weight onto your left foot. Press down firmly through all four corners of your left foot. Find a focal point (drishti)—a stationary object at eye level—to help with balance.

Bend your right knee and rotate it outward. Place the sole of your right foot on your left inner thigh, above the knee (or below the knee on your calf). NEVER place your foot directly on the knee joint—this can injure the knee.

If placing your foot on your inner thigh is too challenging, place the sole of your right foot on your left ankle or calf (below the knee). Alternatively, keep your toes on the floor with your heel resting against your ankle.

Once stable, press your right foot firmly into your left leg (and your left leg back into your right foot). Engage your core to stabilize. On an inhale, bring your palms together at your heart center (Anjali Mudra). For more challenge, inhale and raise your arms overhead with palms facing each other or together.

Hold the pose for 5-10 breaths. Keep your gaze soft but fixed on your drishti. Relax your shoulders away from your ears. Keep your standing leg engaged (not locked). To release, exhale as you lower your arms and right foot. Return to Mountain Pose. Repeat on the opposite side (standing on your right foot, left foot lifted).

If you wobble, that's okay—wobbling means you're learning. Engage your core and find a soft gaze. Imagine roots growing from your standing foot into the earth. Use a wall for support by standing with your back or side against it. The tree grows slowly—be patient with yourself.

common_mistakes.mistake: Placing the foot directly on the knee joint (always place above or below the knee).
Leaning toward the lifted leg (keep your hips level and centered).
Locking the standing knee (keep a micro-bend to protect the joint).
Holding the lifted foot with your hand (use your leg strength to hold it in place).
Looking down (fix your gaze on a stationary point at eye level for better balance).
Holding the breath (breathe deeply and steadily).

Pose.safety: Keep your weight evenly distributed on both feet. Avoid locking your knees—keep a micro-bend. Engage your core to protect your lower back. If you feel dizzy, come out of the pose gently.
Pose.Name: Mountain Pose (Tadasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Full body alignment—spine, core, legs, feet
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Severe vertigo, recent knee surgery, uncontrolled high blood pressure

steps.step: Stand with your feet together or hip-width apart. Distribute your weight evenly across all four corners of your feet.
Engage your thighs, lift your kneecaps slightly (without locking knees). Tuck your tailbone slightly. Lengthen your spine.
Roll your shoulders back and down. Let your arms hang naturally at your sides with palms facing forward or inward.
Keep your chin parallel to the floor. Soften your gaze. Breathe deeply for 30-60 seconds.
Mountain pose looks simple but is the foundation for all standing poses. Imagine a string pulling the crown of your head toward the ceiling.

Pose.safety: Practice near a wall for balance support. Do not place your foot directly on your knee joint. If you have high or low blood pressure, raise your arms only when stable.
Pose.Name: Tree Pose (Vrksasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Standing leg (quadriceps, glutes, calves), core, hip abductors
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee surgery, severe ankle instability, inner ear disorders affecting balance

steps.step: Start in Mountain Pose. Shift weight to your left foot. Find a focal point (drishti) at eye level.
Bend your right knee and place the sole of your right foot on your left inner thigh (above knee) or calf (below knee). Never place foot on the knee joint.
Bring palms together at your heart center. For more challenge, raise arms overhead.
Press your foot into your leg and your leg back into your foot. Hold for 30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
If you wobble, that's okay. Engage your core and soften your gaze. Imagine roots growing from your standing foot into the earth.

common_mistakes.mistake: Placing foot on the knee joint.
Looking down instead of at a drishti.

Pose.safety: Keep your front knee bent at 90 degrees, aligned over your ankle. Do not let your knee extend past your toes. Engage your core to protect your lower back.
Pose.Name: Warrior I (Virabhadrasana I)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Quadriceps, glutes, hamstrings, hip flexors, shoulders, core
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee injury, high blood pressure (uncontrolled), heart conditions

steps.step: Stand at the front of your mat. Step your left foot back 3-4 feet. Turn your left foot out 45 degrees. Square your hips toward the front.
Bend your right knee to 90 degrees, directly over your ankle. Keep your back leg straight and strong.
On an inhale, sweep your arms overhead with palms facing each other or together. Lift your chest.
Keep your shoulders relaxed away from your ears. Gaze forward or slightly up. Hold for 30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Imagine your back foot is pressing firmly into the floor and your front knee is pushing forward like pressing against a wall.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting front knee extend past toes.
Hips not squared to the front.

Pose.safety: Keep your front knee bent at 90 degrees, aligned over your ankle. Open your hips to the side. Keep your shoulders stacked over your hips.
Pose.Name: Warrior II (Virabhadrasana II)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Quadriceps, glutes, hamstrings, inner thighs, shoulders, core
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee injury, high blood pressure (uncontrolled)

steps.step: Stand at the front of your mat. Step your left foot back 3-4 feet. Turn your left foot out 90 degrees, right foot forward.
Bend your right knee to 90 degrees, directly over your ankle. Open your hips to the side (facing the long edge of your mat).
Extend your arms out to the sides at shoulder height, palms down. Gaze over your right fingertips.
Keep your shoulders stacked directly over your hips. Hold for 30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Imagine your arms are being pulled in opposite directions. Press through the outer edge of your back foot.

common_mistakes.mistake: Leaning torso toward the front leg.
Back foot not pressing down firmly.

Pose.safety: Keep your legs straight but not locked. Avoid rounding your back. Reach through both sides of your torso equally. If you have neck issues, keep your gaze forward instead of looking up.
Pose.Name: Triangle Pose (Trikonasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Hamstrings, inner thighs, obliques, spinal muscles, shoulders
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent hamstring tear, lower back herniation, neck injury

steps.step: Stand with feet 3-4 feet apart. Turn your right foot out 90 degrees, left foot in slightly. Align your heels.
Extend your arms out to the sides at shoulder height. Reach forward through your right fingertips.
Hinge at your right hip, lowering your right hand to your shin, ankle, or a block. Reach your left arm straight up toward the ceiling.
Keep both legs straight. Open your chest toward the side. Gaze up at your left thumb or forward. Hold for 30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Imagine your torso is sandwiched between two glass panes—avoid leaning forward or backward. Lengthen through both sides of your waist equally.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting the bottom hand bear weight on the knee.
Looking down instead of up (if neck allows).

Pose.safety: Sit on a folded blanket or block if your hips are tight. Keep your spine tall. Avoid slouching or rounding your back.
Pose.Name: Easy Pose (Sukhasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Hip rotators, spine, core (gentle engagement)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent hip surgery, severe knee arthritis

steps.step: Sit on your mat. Cross your shins, placing each foot under the opposite knee. If needed, elevate your hips with a blanket or block.
Stack your spine tall. Roll your shoulders back and down. Rest your hands on your knees with palms up or down.
Soften your gaze or close your eyes. Relax your jaw and tongue. Breathe naturally for 1-2 minutes.
If one knee sits higher than the other, place a block or folded blanket under the higher knee for support.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting knees float too high without support.
Holding tension in the face or jaw.

Pose.safety: Keep your legs straight but not locked. Engage your thigh muscles. If your lower back rounds, sit on a folded blanket to tilt your pelvis forward.
Pose.Name: Staff Pose (Dandasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Spine, hamstrings, quadriceps, core
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent hamstring tear, lower back herniation

steps.step: Sit on your mat with your legs extended straight in front of you. Sit on a blanket if your lower back rounds.
Press through your heels. Engage your thigh muscles. Point your toes toward the ceiling (flex feet).
Stack your spine tall. Place your hands beside your hips with fingers pointing forward. Press down through your hands.
Lift through the crown of your head. Draw your shoulders back and down. Hold for 30-60 seconds.
Staff pose is the seated equivalent of Mountain Pose. Use it as a foundation for seated forward folds and twists.

common_mistakes.mistake: Pointing toes (flexing feet protects the knees).

Pose.safety: Keep your spine long—hinge from your hips, not your waist. Only go as far as you can with a flat back. Use a strap around your feet if you can't reach your toes.
Pose.Name: Seated Forward Fold (Paschimottanasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Hamstrings, spine, lower back, calves
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent hamstring tear, lower back herniation, sciatica (severe)

steps.step: Sit in Staff Pose (Dandasana). Sit on a blanket if your lower back rounds.
Inhale and lengthen your spine. Reach your arms overhead.
Exhale as you hinge forward from your hips, keeping your back flat. Reach for your feet, shins, or ankles.
If you can, hold your feet with your hands. Let your head relax. Breathe deeply for 30-60 seconds.
Imagine your torso is a flat tabletop folding over your legs. If your hamstrings are tight, bend your knees slightly.

common_mistakes.mistake: Rounding the spine to reach the feet.
Hinging from the waist instead of the hips.

Pose.safety: Do not force your knees toward the floor. If you have knee or groin injuries, place blocks or blankets under your thighs for support.
Pose.Name: Butterfly Pose (Baddha Konasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Hip adductors (inner thighs/groin), hip flexors, lower back
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent groin injury, severe hip arthritis, knee injury

steps.step: Sit on your mat. Bend your knees and bring the soles of your feet together. Let your knees drop out to the sides.
Hold your feet or ankles with your hands. Sit on a blanket if your lower back rounds.
Gently press your knees up and down like butterfly wings for 15-20 flaps.
Keep your spine tall. On an exhale, hinge forward from your hips for a deeper stretch. Hold for 1-2 minutes.
Imagine your knees are heavy and melting toward the floor. Do not push them down with your hands—let gravity work.

Pose.safety: If you have knee or ankle injuries, place a folded blanket under your knees. Rest your forehead on a block if you cannot reach the floor. Avoid this pose if you have diarrhea or recent abdominal surgery.
Pose.Name: Child's Pose (Balasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Lower back, hips, quadriceps, shoulders, neck
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee surgery, late pregnancy (avoid deep pressure on abdomen), diarrhea

steps.step: Kneel on your mat with your knees wide and big toes touching. Sit back on your heels.
Exhale as you fold your torso forward, resting your belly between your thighs.
Rest your forehead on the floor. Extend your arms forward or alongside your body with palms up.
Breathe deeply into your lower back. Hold for 1-2 minutes. To release, walk your hands back and slowly roll up.
Child's pose is a resting pose. Use it anytime you need a break during your practice. Place a block under your forehead if it doesn't reach the floor.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lifting the hips off the heels.
Tension in the neck or shoulders.
Rushing to come out of the pose.

Pose.safety: This is a resting pose. Allow your body to completely relax. If you have lower back discomfort, place a blanket under your knees.
Pose.Name: Corpse Pose (Savasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Full body relaxation—no active muscle engagement
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Late pregnancy (lie on left side instead), severe sleep apnea

steps.step: Lie flat on your back. Let your feet fall open naturally. Place your arms alongside your body with palms facing up.
Tuck your shoulder blades under your chest. Lengthen the back of your neck. Close your eyes.
Systematically relax each part of your body from your toes to the crown of your head.
Let your breath become natural and effortless. Rest for 3-5 minutes. To come out, gently roll to your right side and press yourself up.
Savasana is the most important pose of your practice. It allows your body to integrate the benefits. If your mind wanders, gently bring it back to your breath.

common_mistakes.mistake: Thinking of it as "doing nothing" (it's an active relaxation).
Falling asleep (gentle awareness is the goal).
Rushing to get up at the end.

Pose.safety: Keep both shoulders on the floor. Turn your head in the same direction as your bent knees. Use a blanket under your head if needed.
Pose.Name: Supine Twist (Supta Matsyendrasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Spine (rotators), lower back, chest, shoulders, glutes
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent spinal surgery, herniated disc (severe), osteoporosis

steps.step: Lie on your back. Hug your right knee into your chest.
Cross your right knee over your body to the left side. Extend your right arm out to the side at shoulder height.
Turn your head to look at your right hand. Keep both shoulders on the floor.
Hold for 30 seconds. To release, bring your knee back to center. Repeat on the other side.
Use your left hand on your right knee to gently deepen the twist. Breathe into the side of your torso.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting the top shoulder lift off the floor.
Forcing the twist beyond comfortable range.
Turning head opposite to the twist (keep same side).

Pose.safety: Keep your sacrum (lower back) on the floor. If you have neck or knee issues, support your head with a blanket and avoid pulling on your feet.
Pose.Name: Happy Baby (Ananda Balasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Hip adductors (groin), lower back, hamstrings, shoulders
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee surgery, neck injury, late pregnancy

steps.step: Lie on your back. Bend your knees toward your chest.
Reach your hands between your legs and grip the outsides of your feet (or ankles).
Open your knees wider than your torso, bringing them toward your armpits. Stack your ankles directly over your knees.
Gently pull your feet down with your hands. Rock gently side to side. Hold for 1-2 minutes.
Imagine you are a happy baby playing with your feet. Keep your sacrum on the floor—don't let your lower back lift.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lifting the lower back off the floor.
Knees too far apart (should be aligned with armpits).

Pose.safety: Place a folded blanket under your hips for comfort. Do not force your legs to touch the wall if your hamstrings are tight. If you have high blood pressure, place a pillow under your head.
Pose.Name: Legs Up the Wall (Viparita Karani)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Hamstrings (passive stretch), lower back (release), legs (drainage)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Glaucoma, detached retina, high blood pressure (uncontrolled), recent hip surgery

steps.step: Sit sideways next to a wall. Swing your legs up as you lie back. Your hips should be close to the wall.
Rest your legs vertically against the wall. Place a folded blanket under your hips for comfort if desired.
Rest your arms alongside your body with palms up. Close your eyes.
Breathe deeply and rest for 2-5 minutes. To release, bend your knees and roll to your side.
This pose helps drain fluid from the legs and calms the nervous system. Perfect before bed or after standing all day.

common_mistakes.mistake: Hips too far from the wall.
Tensing the leg muscles (relax and let the wall support you).
Rushing to come out (bend knees and roll slowly).

Pose.safety: Keep your weight in your heels. Do not let your knees extend past your toes. Engage your core to protect your lower back. If you have knee issues, avoid deep bending.
Pose.Name: Chair Pose (Utkatasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Quadriceps, glutes, core, spine, shoulders
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee surgery, patellofemoral pain, lower back herniation

steps.step: Stand in Mountain Pose with feet together or hip-width apart.
Inhale, raise arms overhead. Exhale as you sit back as if sitting in an invisible chair.
Keep your weight in your heels. Do not let your knees extend past your toes.
Keep your back flat, chest lifted. Gaze forward or slightly up. Hold for 20-30 seconds. Release on an exhale.
Imagine you are squeezing a block between your thighs to engage your inner legs. Press your heels down as if trying to lift your toes.

common_mistakes.mistake: Weight shifting to the balls of the feet.

Pose.safety: Practice with a wall or chair for support. Keep your standing leg straight but not locked. Keep your hips level.
Pose.Name: Warrior III (Modified, with Support)
Pose.Equipment: Yoga Mat, Wall or Chair for Support
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Standing leg (glutes, hamstrings, quadriceps), core, spinal extensors
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee injury, ankle instability, high blood pressure (uncontrolled)

steps.step: Stand facing a wall or place hands on a chair. Shift weight to your left foot.
Inhale as you lift your right leg behind you, keeping it straight. Hinge forward from your hips.
Keep your hips level. Your torso, lifted leg, and arms should form a straight line parallel to the floor.
Hold for 20 seconds. To release, lower your leg and return to standing. Repeat on the other side.
Imagine a string pulling your heel back and the crown of your head forward. Keep your standing leg engaged but not locked.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lifting the lifted leg hip higher than the standing leg side.

Pose.safety: Keep your front knee at 90 degrees, aligned over your ankle. Square your hips as much as possible. If you have high blood pressure, keep your arms at heart level instead of overhead.
Pose.Name: Warrior I (Deep - Virabhadrasana I)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Quadriceps, glutes, hamstrings, hip flexors, shoulders, core
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee injury, high blood pressure (uncontrolled)

steps.step: From Mountain Pose, step your left foot back 3-4 feet. Turn your left foot out 45 degrees. Square your hips forward.
Bend your right knee to 90 degrees, directly over your ankle. Keep your back leg straight and strong.
On an inhale, sweep your arms overhead with palms facing each other or together. Lift your chest.
Keep your shoulders relaxed. Gaze forward or slightly up. Hold for 45 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Press the outer edge of your back foot firmly into the mat. Imagine your front knee is pressing against a wall.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting front knee extend past toes.
Hips not squared to the front.

Pose.safety: Keep your front knee at 90 degrees, aligned over your ankle. Open your hips to the side. Keep your shoulders stacked over your hips.
Pose.Name: Warrior II (Deep - Virabhadrasana II)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Quadriceps, glutes, hamstrings, inner thighs, shoulders, core
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee injury, high blood pressure

steps.step: From Mountain Pose, step your left foot back 3-4 feet. Turn your left foot out 90 degrees, right foot forward.
Bend your right knee to 90 degrees, directly over your ankle. Open your hips to the side.
Extend your arms out to the sides at shoulder height, palms down. Gaze over your right fingertips.
Keep your shoulders stacked over your hips. Hold for 45 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Press through the outer edge of your back foot. Imagine your arms are being pulled in opposite directions.

common_mistakes.mistake: Leaning torso toward the front leg.
Back foot not pressing down firmly.

Pose.safety: Keep your standing leg straight but not locked. Keep your hips level. Practice near a wall for balance. If you have high blood pressure, keep your head in a neutral position.
Pose.Name: Warrior III (Full - Virabhadrasana III)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Standing leg (glutes, hamstrings, quadriceps), core, spinal extensors, shoulders
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee injury, ankle instability, high blood pressure

steps.step: From Mountain Pose, shift weight to your left foot. Hinge forward from your hips as you lift your right leg behind you.
Keep both legs straight (but not locked). Your torso, lifted leg, and arms should form a straight line parallel to the floor.
Extend your arms forward alongside your ears, palms facing each other.
Keep your hips level. Engage your core. Gaze at the floor a few feet in front of you. Hold for 30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Imagine a string pulling your lifted heel back and your fingertips forward. Press your standing foot into the floor as if rooting down.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lifting the lifted leg hip higher than the standing leg side.

Pose.safety: Keep your front knee bent at 90 degrees. Reach your top arm over your ear. Keep both sides of your torso equally long.
Pose.Name: Extended Side Angle (Utthita Parsvakonasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Obliques, quadriceps, glutes, hamstrings, shoulders
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee injury, lower back herniation, shoulder impingement

steps.step: From Warrior II, bend your right knee to 90 degrees. Place your right forearm on your right thigh or hand on a block.
Reach your left arm over your left ear, palm facing down. Keep your left leg straight and strong.
Lengthen through your left side ribs. Open your chest toward the ceiling.
Gaze up at your left hand or forward. Hold for 30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Imagine you are between two panes of glass—avoid leaning forward or backward. Press through the outer edge of your back foot.

common_mistakes.mistake: Collapsing the chest toward the front leg.
Letting the front knee collapse inward.
Top arm dropping forward instead of reaching over ear.

Pose.safety: Practice near a wall for balance. Keep your standing leg straight but not locked. Open your hips to the side. If you have neck issues, keep your gaze forward.
Pose.Name: Half Moon Pose (Ardha Chandrasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Standing leg (glutes, hamstrings, quadriceps), core, obliques, shoulders
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee surgery, ankle instability, high blood pressure

steps.step: From Triangle Pose, bend your right knee. Place your right hand on the floor or a block in front of your right foot.
Shift weight onto your right leg. Inhale as you lift your left leg parallel to the floor. Flex your left foot.
Open your left hip to the side. Stack your left shoulder over your right shoulder. Gaze up at your left hand or forward.
Keep your standing leg straight (but not locked). Hold for 30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Place a block under your hand for support. Imagine a wall behind your lifted leg—press your heel into it.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting the lifted leg hip drop.

Pose.safety: Keep your front knee at 90 degrees, aligned over your ankle. Square your hips forward. If you have knee issues, place a blanket under your back knee.
Pose.Name: Crescent Lunge (Anjaneyasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Quadriceps, glutes, hip flexors, hamstrings, shoulders, core
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee injury, quadriceps tear

steps.step: From Downward Dog, step your right foot forward between your hands. Lower your left knee to the floor (or keep it lifted for more challenge).
Bend your right knee to 90 degrees, directly over your ankle. Square your hips forward.
On an inhale, sweep your arms overhead with palms facing each other. Lift your chest.
Keep your shoulders relaxed. Gaze forward or slightly up. Hold for 30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Keep your front knee tracking over your second toe. Press the top of your back foot into the mat.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting front knee extend past toes.

Pose.safety: Keep your knees tracking over your toes. Sink your hips low. Keep your spine tall. If you have knee issues, avoid deep bending.
Pose.Name: Goddess Pose (Utkata Konasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Quadriceps, glutes, inner thighs, core, shoulders
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee surgery, hip impingement

steps.step: Stand with feet wide (3-4 feet apart). Turn your toes outward 45 degrees.
Bend your knees, sinking your hips low. Keep your knees tracking over your second toes.
Bend your elbows to 90 degrees with palms facing forward (like cactus arms). Lift your chest.
Keep your spine tall. Engage your core. Hold for 30-45 seconds.
Imagine you are pressing your feet into the floor and drawing energy up through your legs. Keep your shoulders relaxed.

Pose.safety: Keep your extended leg straight but not locked. Hinge from your hips, not your waist. Only go as far as you can with a flat back.
Pose.Name: Head-to-Knee Pose (Janu Sirsasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Hamstrings, spine, lower back, hip flexors
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent hamstring tear, lower back herniation

steps.step: Sit in Staff Pose. Bend your right knee and place the sole of your right foot against your left inner thigh.
Inhale and reach your arms overhead. Exhale as you hinge forward over your left leg.
Hold your left foot, shin, or ankle. Keep your left leg straight and flexed. Hold for 30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
If you can't reach your foot, use a strap around your foot. Keep your shoulders relaxed and away from your ears.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lifting the bent knee off the floor.

Pose.safety: Keep your spine tall. Turn your torso from your waist, not just your head. Keep both sit bones grounded.
Pose.Name: Seated Twist (Ardha Matsyendrasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Spine (rotators), obliques, shoulders, neck
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent spinal surgery, herniated disc (severe)

steps.step: Sit in Staff Pose. Bend your right knee and place your right foot outside your left thigh.
Bend your left knee and bring your left heel toward your right hip.
Inhale to lengthen your spine. Exhale as you twist to the right, placing your left elbow outside your right knee.
Place your right hand behind you for support. Gaze over your right shoulder. Hold for 30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Lengthen your spine on each inhale, twist deeper on each exhale. Keep both sit bones grounded.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lifting the sit bone opposite the twist.
Using arm strength instead of core to twist.
Forcing the twist beyond comfortable range.

Pose.safety: Keep your legs straight but not locked. Hinge from your hips. If your lower back rounds, sit on a blanket. Only go as far as comfortable.
Pose.Name: Wide-Legged Forward Fold (Upavistha Konasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Hamstrings, inner thighs, spine, lower back
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent hamstring tear, lower back herniation, groin injury

steps.step: Sit in Staff Pose. Open your legs wide to a comfortable V-shape. Sit on a blanket if your lower back rounds.
Inhale and reach your arms overhead. Exhale as you hinge forward from your hips.
Walk your hands forward along the floor between your legs. Keep your back flat as long as possible.
When you feel a stretch, relax your head. Hold for 45 seconds. To release, walk your hands back and slowly roll up.
Keep your toes pointing toward the ceiling and your legs engaged. Use blocks under your hands if you can't reach the floor.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting legs rotate outward (keep toes pointing up).

Pose.safety: Keep your spine straight—do not round. Engage your core. If you have lower back issues, keep your knees bent slightly.
Pose.Name: Boat Pose (Navasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Core (rectus abdominis, transverse abdominis), hip flexors, spine
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Lower back herniation, recent abdominal surgery

steps.step: Sit on your mat with your knees bent and feet flat. Lean back slightly, balancing on your sit bones.
Lift your feet off the floor, bringing your shins parallel to the floor. Keep your knees bent or straighten for more challenge.
Extend your arms forward alongside your ears, parallel to the floor.
Keep your spine straight and chest lifted. Hold for 20-30 seconds. To release, lower your feet gently.
If you feel your lower back rounding, bend your knees more or lower your feet. Imagine balancing a cup of tea on your shins.

Pose.safety: Keep your front knee bent at 90 degrees. Square your hips forward. If you have knee issues, place a blanket under your bent knee. Do not force the stretch.
Pose.Name: Pigeon Pose (Eka Pada Rajakapotasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: External hip rotators (piriformis), glutes, hip flexors
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee injury, sacroiliac joint dysfunction, hip impingement

steps.step: From Downward Dog, bring your right knee forward toward your right wrist. Place your right shin on the floor at an angle.
Extend your left leg straight behind you, keeping the top of your foot on the floor. Square your hips forward.
On an exhale, lower your torso over your right leg. Rest on your forearms or fold forward completely.
Hold for 45 seconds. To release, press through your hands and lift your torso. Repeat on the other side.
If your right hip lifts off the floor, place a blanket under it for support. Flex your back foot to protect your knee.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting the front hip lift off the floor.
Rounding the spine when folding forward.
Turning the back foot out (keep top of foot on floor).
Forcing the shin parallel to the front of the mat.

Pose.safety: Keep your knees aligned over your ankles. Press through your feet and shoulders. Do not lift your hips higher than comfortable. If you have neck issues, place a blanket under your shoulders.
Pose.Name: Bridge Pose (Setu Bandha Sarvangasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Glutes, hamstrings, lower back, chest, shoulders, neck
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent neck injury, spinal fusion, severe osteoporosis

steps.step: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart. Place your arms alongside your body, palms down.
Press through your feet and arms. Inhale as you lift your hips toward the ceiling.
Interlace your fingers under your back and press your arms into the floor.
Roll your shoulders under your chest. Lift your chest toward your chin. Hold for 30-45 seconds. Exhale as you lower.
Keep your knees aligned over your ankles. Imagine you are sliding a block under your sacrum—maintain that lift.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lifting hips too high, compressing the lower back.

Pose.safety: Keep your neck long and relaxed. Lift your chest and legs simultaneously. Do not lift higher than comfortable. If you have lower back issues, lift only your chest or legs, not both.
Pose.Name: Locust Pose (Salabhasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Lower back (erector spinae), glutes, hamstrings, shoulders
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent spinal surgery, herniated disc, severe osteoporosis

steps.step: Lie face down on your mat with arms alongside your body, palms down. Place your forehead on the floor.
Inhale as you lift your chest, arms, and legs off the floor. Keep your neck long—gaze at the floor.
Reach through your fingers and toes. Squeeze your glutes and engage your lower back.
Hold for 20-30 seconds. Exhale as you lower with control.
Keep your legs straight and engaged. Do not lift higher than comfortable—a small lift is effective.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lifting the head too high (strain the neck).

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows hugged close to your body. Lift your chest without straining your lower back. If you have wrist issues, place your forearms on the floor instead of hands.
Pose.Name: Cobra Pose (Bhujangasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Spine (extensors), chest, shoulders, glutes, core
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent spinal surgery, herniated disc, carpal tunnel syndrome (severe)

steps.step: Lie face down with legs together. Place your hands under your shoulders, elbows hugged close to your body.
Press gently into your hands. Inhale as you lift your chest off the floor. Keep your pelvis and legs on the floor.
Engage your glutes and lower back. Keep your shoulders relaxed away from your ears.
Gaze forward or slightly up without straining your neck. Hold for 20-30 seconds. Exhale as you lower.
Keep your elbows hugging your ribs. Do not push into your hands—use your back muscles to lift.

common_mistakes.mistake: Pushing into hands instead of using back muscles.
Lifting the head too high (strain the neck).
Lifting the pelvis off the floor.

Pose.safety: Keep your knees hip-width apart. Lift your chest and legs simultaneously. If you have lower back issues, avoid lifting too high or practice with a blanket under your pelvis.
Pose.Name: Bow Pose (Dhanurasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Full back (upper, middle, lower), glutes, hamstrings, chest, shoulders
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent spinal surgery, herniated disc, high blood pressure, pregnancy

steps.step: Lie face down with knees hip-width apart. Bend your knees and reach your hands back to hold your ankles.
Inhale as you lift your chest and thighs off the floor. Press your feet into your hands.
Gently rock forward onto your belly. Keep your gaze forward.
Hold for 20-30 seconds. Exhale as you release. Rest in Child's Pose.
Keep your knees hip-width apart. Imagine your body is a bow—your torso and legs are the bow, your hands are the string.

Pose.safety: Practice near a wall for balance. Keep your standing leg engaged but not locked. Keep your hips level.
Pose.Name: Tree Pose (Deep - Vrksasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Standing leg (quadriceps, glutes, calves), core, hip abductors
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee surgery, severe ankle instability

steps.step: Start in Mountain Pose. Shift weight to your left foot. Find a drishti at eye level.
Place your right foot high on your left inner thigh (above the knee). Press your foot into your thigh and your thigh back into your foot.
Bring palms together at heart center or raise arms overhead.
Engage your core. Keep your standing leg engaged. Hold for 45 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
To improve balance, focus on a stationary point. Imagine your standing foot has four corners all pressing into the floor.

common_mistakes.mistake: Placing foot on the knee joint.

Pose.safety: Keep your standing leg straight but not locked. Wrap your arms and legs as much as comfortable. If you have knee issues, practice without wrapping the lifted leg.
Pose.Name: Eagle Pose (Garudasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Standing leg (glutes, quadriceps, calves), core, shoulders, hip abductors
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee surgery, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Start in Mountain Pose. Shift weight to your left foot. Bend your right knee and cross your right thigh over your left.
Hook your right foot behind your left calf (or touch toes to floor for balance).
Cross your right arm under your left. Bend elbows and wrap forearms, pressing palms together.
Sink your hips slightly. Lift your elbows. Hold for 30 seconds. Unwind and repeat on the other side.
Keep your gaze soft. Imagine your elbows are lifting toward the ceiling as your hips sink.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting the wrapped leg hip drop.

Pose.safety: Practice near a wall for balance. Keep your standing leg engaged. Reach your lifted leg back as far as comfortable. If you have lower back issues, avoid leaning too far forward.
Pose.Name: Dancer Pose (Modified - Natarajasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Standing leg (glutes, quadriceps), core, quadriceps (lifted leg), shoulders, chest
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee surgery, shoulder impingement, lower back herniation

steps.step: Stand in Mountain Pose. Shift weight to your left foot. Bend your right knee, bringing your right heel toward your glute.
Reach back with your right hand and hold the inside of your right foot or ankle.
Press your foot into your hand as you lift your right leg behind you. Extend your left arm forward for balance.
Keep your standing leg engaged. Gaze forward. Hold for 20 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Use a strap around your foot if you can't reach it. Keep your hips level—don't tilt toward the lifted leg.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting the lifted leg hip rise.
Lifting the shoulder toward the ear.

Pose.safety: If you have high blood pressure, glaucoma, or recent stroke, avoid this pose. Keep your weight in your heels. Do not let your shoulders collapse.
Pose.Name: Downward Dog (Adho Mukha Svanasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Hamstrings, calves, shoulders, spine, core, arms
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: High blood pressure (uncontrolled), glaucoma, recent stroke, carpal tunnel syndrome

steps.step: Start on hands and knees. Place hands shoulder-width apart, knees under hips.
Tuck your toes. Exhale as you lift your hips toward the ceiling, straightening your legs.
Press your palms into the mat. Draw your shoulders away from your ears. Keep your head between your arms.
Bend your knees slightly if your hamstrings are tight. Pedal your feet to stretch each calf. Hold for 45-60 seconds.
Imagine your hips are being pulled back by a string. Keep your spine long—don't round your upper back.

Pose.safety: If you have high blood pressure, glaucoma, or shoulder injuries, avoid this pose or practice with caution. Keep your weight in your forearms, not your head.
Pose.Name: Dolphin Pose
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Shoulders, upper back, hamstrings, calves, core
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: High blood pressure (uncontrolled), glaucoma, recent shoulder injury, carpal tunnel

steps.step: Start on hands and knees. Lower onto your forearms, elbows shoulder-width apart. Interlace your fingers or keep palms flat.
Tuck your toes. Exhale as you lift your hips toward the ceiling, straightening your legs.
Press your forearms into the mat. Draw your shoulders away from your ears. Let your head hang between your arms.
Keep your spine long. Hold for 30-45 seconds. To release, lower your knees to the floor.
Dolphin pose builds shoulder strength for headstands. Keep your elbows directly under your shoulders.

Pose.safety: Use a wall for support. Place a folded blanket under your hips for comfort. Do not force your legs to touch the wall. Avoid if you have high blood pressure or glaucoma.
Pose.Name: Legs Up the Wall (Extended - Viparita Karani)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Hamstrings (passive), lower back (release), legs (circulation)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Glaucoma, high blood pressure (uncontrolled), detached retina

steps.step: Sit sideways next to a wall. Swing your legs up as you lie back. Your hips should be close to the wall.
Rest your legs vertically against the wall. Place a folded blanket under your hips for comfort if desired.
Rest your arms alongside your body with palms up. Close your eyes.
Breathe deeply and rest for 3-5 minutes. To release, bend your knees and roll to your side.
This pose helps calm the nervous system. Perfect before bed or after standing all day.

common_mistakes.mistake: Hips too far from the wall.

Pose.safety: This is a deep twist. Keep your standing leg straight but not locked. Do not force the twist—breathe into it. If you have lower back issues, avoid or practice with caution.
Pose.Name: Revolved Triangle (Parivrtta Trikonasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Hamstrings, obliques, spine (rotators), shoulders, hips
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent lower back herniation, spinal fusion, hamstring tear

steps.step: Stand with feet 3-4 feet apart. Turn your right foot out 90 degrees, left foot in slightly.
Hinge forward from your hips, keeping your back flat. Place your left hand on the floor or a block inside your right foot.
Twist your torso to the right, reaching your right arm straight up toward the ceiling.
Keep both legs straight. Gaze up at your right hand. Hold for 45 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Imagine your torso is being twisted like a washcloth—keep length in your spine as you twist.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting the bottom hand bear weight on the knee.

Pose.safety: This is an advanced balance pose. Practice near a wall. Keep your standing leg engaged. If you feel dizzy, come out slowly.
Pose.Name: Revolved Half Moon (Parivrtta Ardha Chandrasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Standing leg (glutes, hamstrings), core, obliques, shoulders
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent lower back injury, hamstring tear, high blood pressure

steps.step: From Revolved Triangle, bend your front knee. Shift weight onto your front foot as you lift your back leg.
Balance on your front leg. Keep your bottom hand on the floor or a block.
Reach your top arm straight up, then twist it across your body to open your chest.
Keep your hips level. Gaze up or down. Hold for 30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Use a wall for balance. Keep your lifted leg active—press through your heel.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting the lifted leg hip drop.
Looking up if it strains the neck.

Pose.safety: Keep your standing leg straight but not locked. Hinge from your hips. If you have high blood pressure, keep your head in a neutral position.
Pose.Name: Standing Split (Urdhva Prasarita Eka Padasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Standing leg (hamstrings, glutes), lifted leg (hamstrings, hip flexors), core
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent hamstring tear, lower back herniation, high blood pressure

steps.step: From Mountain Pose, hinge forward from your hips. Place your hands on the floor or blocks.
Shift weight onto your left foot. Inhale as you lift your right leg straight up toward the ceiling.
Square your hips toward the floor. Keep both legs straight.
Walk your hands back toward your standing foot. Hold for 30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
Keep your standing leg engaged. Imagine your lifted leg is pressing into a wall behind you.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting the lifted leg hip rise.

Pose.safety: Do not force the lotus position. Only attempt if you have open hips. If you feel knee pain, come out immediately. Practice on a soft surface.
Pose.Name: Full Lotus (Padmasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Hip rotators, knees, ankles, spine
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee surgery, hip replacement, ankle injury, sciatica

steps.step: Sit on your mat. Bend your right knee and place your right foot on top of your left thigh.
Bend your left knee and place your left foot on top of your right thigh.
Stack your spine tall. Rest your hands on your knees with palms up or down.
Breathe deeply. Hold for 3-5 minutes. To release, slowly unfold one leg at a time.
If you cannot sit in Full Lotus, practice Half Lotus (one foot on opposite thigh). Never force your knees.

common_mistakes.mistake: Forcing the knees to the floor.

Pose.safety: Warm up thoroughly before attempting. If you have wrist, shoulder, or lower back issues, avoid. Practice on a soft surface. Come out slowly if you feel dizziness.
Pose.Name: Wheel Pose (Urdhva Dhanurasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Full spine (extensors), shoulders, chest, glutes, hamstrings, quadriceps, wrists
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Carpal tunnel syndrome, shoulder impingement, lower back herniation, high blood pressure

steps.step: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart. Place your hands beside your ears, fingers pointing toward your shoulders.
Press into your hands and feet. Inhale as you lift your hips and head off the floor.
Straighten your arms and legs as much as comfortable. Keep your knees aligned over your ankles.
Hold for 20-30 seconds. To release, lower slowly. Rest in Child's Pose.
Keep your elbows parallel—don't let them flare out. Press your chest toward the wall behind you.

Pose.safety: Practice on a soft surface. Place a blanket in front of you to cushion your head. Engage your core. If you feel wrist pain, come out.
Pose.Name: Crow Pose (Bakasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Core, arms (triceps, biceps), shoulders, wrists, hip flexors
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Carpal tunnel syndrome, wrist injury, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Start in a squat with feet hip-width apart. Place your hands on the floor shoulder-width apart.
Place your knees on the backs of your upper arms, as high as possible.
Lean forward, shifting weight into your hands. Engage your core.
Lift your feet off the floor, bringing your heels toward your glutes. Gaze forward. Hold for 10-20 seconds.
Keep your gaze forward, not down. If you're falling, place a blanket under your face for confidence.

Pose.safety: Practice against a wall first. Do not attempt if you have neck, shoulder, or back injuries. Keep your weight on your forearms, not your head. Have a spotter.
Pose.Name: Supported Headstand (Salamba Sirsasana)
Pose.Equipment: Yoga Mat, Wall (for support), Blanket (for head)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Neck, shoulders, core, spine, legs (full body integration)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Neck injury, high blood pressure, glaucoma, recent stroke, spinal issues

steps.step: Kneel on your mat. Place your forearms on the floor, interlace your fingers. Place the crown of your head on the floor, cradled by your hands.
Lift your hips, straightening your legs. Walk your feet toward your head.
Engage your core. Lift one leg at a time, bringing both legs straight up.
Keep your weight on your forearms, not your head. Hold for 30-60 seconds. To release, lower legs slowly.
Do not turn your head in headstand. Practice with a wall behind you. If you feel neck pressure, come out immediately.

common_mistakes.mistake: Putting weight on the head instead of forearms.

Pose.safety: Place folded blankets under your shoulders to protect your neck. Keep your weight on your shoulders, not your neck. If you have neck or shoulder issues, avoid.
Pose.Name: Supported Shoulderstand (Salamba Sarvangasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Neck (stabilizers), shoulders, core, spine, legs, thyroid gland
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Neck injury, high blood pressure, glaucoma, detached retina, pregnancy

steps.step: Place folded blankets on your mat. Lie on your back with your shoulders on the blankets and head on the mat.
Press your palms into the floor. Inhale as you lift your legs straight up to 90 degrees.
Press into your arms and lift your hips off the floor. Support your lower back with your hands.
Walk your hands up your back toward your shoulder blades. Straighten your legs toward the ceiling.
Keep your weight on your shoulders, not your neck. Do not turn your head. After the pose, rest in Fish Pose (Matsyasana).

Pose.safety: This is a deep standing balance. Practice against a wall. Keep your standing leg engaged. If you have high blood pressure or neck issues, keep your gaze forward.
Pose.Name: Sugarcane Pose (Ardha Chandra Chapasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Standing leg (glutes, hamstrings, quadriceps), core, quadriceps (lifted leg), shoulders, chest
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Recent knee surgery, shoulder impingement, lower back herniation

steps.step: Start in Half Moon Pose (Ardha Chandrasana) on your right leg.
Bend your left knee, bringing your left heel toward your glute.
Reach your left hand back to hold your left foot. Press your foot into your hand.
Reach your right arm forward. Open your chest toward the side. Gaze at your right hand.
This pose looks like a sugarcane stalk. Keep your standing leg engaged. Use a wall for balance.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting the lifted leg hip drop.

Pose.safety: This is an advanced arm balance and hip opener. Practice on a soft surface. Use blocks for support. Do not attempt without proper warm-up.
Pose.Name: Firefly Pose (Full - Tittibhasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Core, arms (triceps), shoulders, hip adductors, hamstrings, wrists
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Carpal tunnel syndrome, wrist injury, shoulder impingement, lower back herniation

steps.step: Squat with feet wider than hips. Thread your hands under your legs, palms flat on the floor.
Press into your hands. Engage your core. Lift your feet off the floor.
Straighten your legs out to the sides as much as comfortable.
Keep your gaze forward. Hold for 20-30 seconds. Release gently.
This pose requires open hamstrings and hip adductors. Practice with blocks under your hands for height.

Pose.safety: This is a deep backbend. Warm up thoroughly with Camel, Wheel, and Bridge poses. If you feel sharp pain in your lower back or knees, come out. Practice on a soft surface.
Pose.Name: King Pigeon (Kapotasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Full spine (extensors), hip flexors, quadriceps, shoulders, chest
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Lower back herniation, knee injury, shoulder impingement, high blood pressure

steps.step: From Downward Dog, bring your right knee forward toward your right wrist. Extend your left leg straight behind you.
Square your hips forward. Place your hands on the floor or on your right knee.
Bend your left knee, reaching your left foot toward your head. Reach your left hand back to hold your foot.
Press your foot into your hand. Lift your chest. Gaze forward or up. Hold for 20-30 seconds. Repeat on the other side.
If you cannot reach your foot, use a strap around your foot. Keep your front knee aligned over your hip.

Pose.safety: Practice on a soft surface. Warm up your wrists and shoulders. If you feel wrist pain, come out. Engage your core. Have a spotter or wall for confidence.
Pose.Name: Peacock Pose (Mayurasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Core (entire abdominal wall), arms (triceps, biceps), shoulders, wrists
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Carpal tunnel syndrome, wrist injury, elbow tendinitis, pregnancy

steps.step: Kneel on your mat. Place your hands on the floor with fingers pointing toward your knees.
Press your elbows together against your belly. Lean forward, placing your belly on your elbows.
Extend your legs straight behind you. Engage your core.
Press into your hands. Lift your legs off the floor. Hold for 10-20 seconds. To release, lower gently.
Peacock pose is an advanced core strengthener. Practice with a blanket under your face for confidence.

Pose.safety: This is a full inversion. Practice against a wall. Warm up your shoulders and core. Do not attempt if you have wrist, shoulder, or neck injuries.
Pose.Name: Freestanding Handstand (Adho Mukha Vrksasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Shoulders, core, wrists, spine, legs (full body integration)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Wrist injury, shoulder impingement, high blood pressure, glaucoma, neck injury

steps.step: Start in Downward Dog facing a wall. Walk your feet up the wall until your hips are over your shoulders.
Shift weight into your hands. Lift one leg off the wall, then the other.
Find balance by pressing through your hands. Engage your core. Gaze between your hands.
Hold for 10-30 seconds. To release, lower one leg at a time back to the wall or floor.
Build handstand strength against a wall first. Keep your shoulders stacked over your wrists. Don't arch your back.

common_mistakes.mistake: Looking at the floor (look between hands).

Pose.safety: This is an advanced inversion. Practice against a wall. Warm up your shoulders and core. Do not attempt if you have neck, shoulder, or back injuries.
Pose.Name: Forearm Stand (Freestanding - Pincha Mayurasana)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Shoulders, core, upper back, wrists, spine
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, neck injury, high blood pressure, glaucoma

steps.step: Kneel on your mat. Place your forearms on the floor, elbows shoulder-width apart. Interlace your fingers.
Lift your hips, straightening your legs. Walk your feet toward your head.
Engage your core. Lift one leg, then the other, into a vertical position.
Press through your forearms. Keep your shoulders stacked over your elbows. Gaze between your hands.
Practice against a wall first. Keep your core engaged to prevent backbending. A slight hollow body position helps balance.
